generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components (Active Digital Twin components)
instance ManufacturingProcessController : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Active Digital Twin component providing autonomous data processing and bidirectional communication with physical manufacturing systems, enabling real-time adjustments based on contextual data traces."
]

instance SimulationEngineController : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Component managing simulation workflows across multiple abstraction layers (requirements → functional/logical architecture → technical implementation) using tools like MathWorks Simulink and state machine automata."
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance OperationalSensorArray : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Passive data collector aggregating contextual operational logs and state traces from physical manufacturing systems for digital shadow representation."
]

instance EnvironmentalConditionSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor collecting environmental metrics (temperature, pressure, motion) for contextual data aggregation in manufacturing monitoring applications."
]

instance ProcessParameterSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Specialized sensor measuring process-specific parameters (e.g., machine state, operational efficiency) for high-fidelity digital twin representation."
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance ContextualDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom OperationalSensorArray, EnvironmentalConditionSensor, ProcessParameterSensor
]

instance OperationalLogTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom OperationalSensorArray
]

instance ProcessStateTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ProcessParameterSensor
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance FunctionalArchitectureModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML/ADL-based model defining component interfaces and interconnections at functional/logical architecture level using ACME, AADL, or MontiArc notation."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationEngineController
]

instance BehavioralSimulationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Simulation engine model capturing dynamic system behaviors through state machines (SysML v1.7) or MontiArc Automata, integrated with MathWorks Simulink."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationEngineController
]

instance TechnicalImplementationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Geometric, analytical, and behavioral models at product/technical layer implementing physics-based simulations in C++/Java."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationEngineController
]

instance RequirementsSpecification : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Functional and logical architecture requirements defined via RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT methodologies."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo FunctionalArchitectureModel
]

instance ContextualDataRepository : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated contextual data traces from multiple sensors for digital shadow representation, processed through active components."
    DTDFVocab:fromData ContextualDataTrace
]

instance OperationalLogData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Collected operational logs from physical systems, filtered and normalized for specific manufacturing monitoring purposes."
    DTDFVocab:fromData OperationalLogTrace
]

instance ProcessStateData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Process-specific state traces capturing machine operational parameters for high-fidelity twin representation."
    DTDFVocab:fromData ProcessStateTrace
]

// C11: Enablers
instance MBSEFramework : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Model-Based Systems Engineering framework integrating SysML, ADLs (AADL, MontiArc), and multi-paradigm methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM) for structured system modeling across abstraction layers."
]

instance SimulationEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Simulation engine platform (MathWorks Simulink) enabling dynamic behavior modeling with state machine automata for cross-layer validation."
    DTDFVocab:enables BehavioralSimulationModel
]

instance CodeImplementation : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "General-purpose programming languages (C++/Java) for low-level system implementation and integration with simulation components."
    DTDFVocab:enables TechnicalImplementationModel
]

instance DigitalShadow : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Passive, one-way data aggregation layer (Digital Shadow Reference Model) collecting contextual traces from physical systems for digital representation."
    DTDFVocab:enables ContextualDataRepository
]

instance StakeholderIntegration : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Tooling supporting heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives through integrated modeling workflows (SysML, ADLs) and multi-modeling notations."
    DTDFVocab:enables RequirementsSpecification
]

// C6: Services
instance DataAggregationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service aggregating and abstracting raw sensor data traces into structured forms for specific manufacturing monitoring applications."
    DTDFVocab:provides ContextualDataRepository
]

instance SimulationValidationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service validating cross-layer model consistency through behavioral and physical simulation cross-referencing."
    DTDFVocab:provides FunctionalArchitectureModel, BehavioralSimulationModel
]

instance ProcessMonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service providing real-time process state monitoring using specialized sensor data and active processing components."
    DTDFVocab:provides ProcessStateData
]

instance ArchitectureCompositionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service managing component composition relationships via ADLs (MontiArc, SysML IBDs) for structured system architecture."
    DTDFVocab:provides FunctionalArchitectureModel
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance OperationalEfficiencyInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time insight derived from process state data indicating current operational efficiency metrics relative to target performance."
]

instance AnomalyDetectionInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Detected deviations from normal operational patterns identified through contextual data analysis and simulation validation."
]

instance ProcessOptimizationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Autonomous adjustment action triggered by ManufacturingProcessController based on efficiency insights and process state anomalies."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance SimulationRefinementAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Iterative refinement action for simulation models triggered by validation service indicating model discrepancies."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance ArchitectureValidationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Cross-layer validation action ensuring model consistency between functional/logical and technical implementation layers."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

// C17: Environment
instance ManufacturingSystemEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains OperationalSensorArray, EnvironmentalConditionSensor, ProcessParameterSensor, ManufacturingProcessController
]

instance DigitalTwinInfrastructure : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains FunctionalArchitectureModel, BehavioralSimulationModel, TechnicalImplementationModel,
        ContextualDataRepository, OperationalLogData, ProcessStateData,
        MBSEFramework, SimulationEngine, CodeImplementation, DigitalShadow, StakeholderIntegration
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The provided documents do not explicitly detail synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity for Digital Twin (DT) systems. However, the distinction between **Digital Shadows** and **Digital Twins** implies that while Digital Shadows operate as **one-way, passive data flows** (e.g., contextual data traces aggregated for specific purposes), Digital Twins introduce **active components** that interpret and process incoming data autonomously. This suggests that DTs may require **real-time or near-real-time synchronization** (e.g., sub-second or millisecond latency) to maintain coherence between physical and digital states, though exact time scales are not specified. The documents also mention **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** requiring integration across multiple abstraction layers (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers), implying **asynchronous or event-driven temporal handling** for interoperability. No specific tools or methodologies are detailed for managing these temporal aspects in DTs beyond the passive nature of Digital Shadows."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The **Digital Twin constellation** is not explicitly defined in the documents, but the context implies an **orchestrated system of interconnected Digital Twins and Digital Shadows**. The **Digital Shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) outlines a UML-based class diagram for manufacturing contexts, suggesting a **component-and-connector perspective** (e.g., via ADLs like MontiArc or AADL) for integrating DTs. The **active components** in DTs (as opposed to passive Digital Shadows) would require **system-wide coordination mechanisms**, likely implemented through **architecture description languages (ADLs)** for defining interconnections, interfaces, and resource management. The **multi-layered modeling approach** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) implies a **hierarchical orchestration** where DTs coordinate across abstraction layers (e.g., functional, logical, physical). No specific orchestration patterns (e.g., microservices, edge computing) or resource management tools (e.g., Kubernetes, Simulink) are mentioned, but the reliance on **simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink)** suggests real-time or event-driven coordination. The **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** would necessitate **standardized interfaces** (e.g., SysML state machines, MontiArc automata) for seamless DT constellation operation."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
    A Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the described approach is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, spanning **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The lifecycle is segmented into distinct abstraction levels, with transitions managed via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs**, which define component interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior is further detailed using **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automata**, often integrated with simulation tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages (**C++/Java**) for validation. The lifecycle governance involves **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-notation modeling** to ensure consistency across tools and perspectives, reflecting a **multi-phase, abstraction-driven evolution** that aligns with **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT** frameworks.
  "
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
    **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
    Fidelity is ensured through **multi-layered validation** across MBSE abstraction levels (e.g., requirements → behavior → physical simulation). Key methods include:
    - **Validation/Verification**: Cross-referenced via **ADLs (e.g., MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines)** and **simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink) to align digital and physical behaviors.
    - **Uncertainty Quantification**: Implicitly addressed by **aggregating contextual data traces** (Digital Shadow Model [32]) without explicit error bounds or probabilistic models.
    - **Error Detection/Correction**: Relies on **model consistency checks** between components (e.g., via ADL connectors) and **behavioral simulation discrepancies**.
    - **Quality Assurance**: Enforced via **stakeholder-integrated MBSE workflows** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) to ensure traceability and alignment across abstraction layers.
    **Note**: No explicit mention of **uncertainty propagation**, **sensitivity analysis**, or **machine learning-based validation** in the provided context.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **Digital Twin (DT)** infrastructure implicitly relies on:
- **Communication protocols**: Not explicitly detailed, but assumes integration with **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) tools** (e.g., SysML, AADL) and simulation environments (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools) for bidirectional data exchange.
- **Network topology/architecture**: Not specified, but implies a **heterogeneous integration** of models across different abstraction layers (e.g., requirements → logical architecture → product layer) via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like MontiArc or SysML IBDs.
- **Bandwidth/latency**: Not quantified, but the focus is on **complexity management** via layered modeling (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) to handle interconnections between components and behaviors.
- **Reliability/redundancy**: Not addressed; the documents emphasize **active components** (e.g., in digital shadows) to process incoming data but do not specify redundancy mechanisms.
- **Security protocols**: Not mentioned; the context centers on **modeling methodologies** and data aggregation rather than cybersecurity.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
The **Digital Twin system** deployment is conceptualized through:
- **Hosting platforms**: Not explicitly cloud/edge/on-premise; however, the **MBSE framework** supports integration across diverse environments (e.g., cloud-based simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink or on-premise ADLs like AADL).
- **Computational/storage requirements**: Not quantified; the focus is on **modeling complexity** via layered architectures (e.g., SysML, RFLP) and heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives.
- **Scalability mechanisms**: Implicitly addressed by **multi-layered modeling** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) to manage scalability across components, behaviors, and abstraction levels.
- **Deployment models**: Not specified; the documents describe **methodologies** (e.g., SMArDT) rather than deployment architectures.
- **Availability/reliability assurance**: Not detailed; the emphasis is on **model integration** and **active processing** (e.g., digital shadows) rather than uptime guarantees.
"
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
    The described Digital Twin system adheres to **industry-standard modeling frameworks** such as **SysML, AADL, and MontiArc**, which define **component/interface standards** for system description. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** like ACME, AADL, and MontiArc provide **interoperability protocols** for component composition and behavior modeling. **Digital Shadows** are distinguished by their **one-way data flow** (passive aggregation of contextual data traces) and require **active processing components** (e.g., software interpreters) to enable bidirectional interaction. While no explicit certification or compliance frameworks are mentioned, the system’s reliance on **MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT)** and **simulation tools (Simulink, C++)** implies adherence to **engineering best practices** and **industry-adopted modeling standards** for interoperability and validation.
  "
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
    **Security and Safety Considerations**:

    **1. Cybersecurity Protocols**:
    The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework, as referenced in [Document 50](https://www.plattform-i40.de/IP/Redaktion/EN/Downloads/Publikation/Details_of_the_Asset_Administration_Shell_Part1_V2.html), integrates **industrial cybersecurity best practices** to ensure secure data exchange and system integrity. While specific protocols like **MQTT** (as mentioned in [Document 52] for IoT communication) are implied for lightweight, low-latency messaging within digital twin ecosystems, the documents do not explicitly detail encryption standards (e.g., TLS 1.3) or authentication mechanisms (e.g., OAuth 2.0) for AAS interactions. However, the **RAMI 4.0** architecture ([Document 48] and [Document 49]) emphasizes **secure data flows** across layers (e.g., physical, cyber-physical, digital), suggesting adherence to **ISO/IEC 27001** or **IEC 62443** standards for cybersecurity compliance.

    **2. Access Control Mechanisms**:
    The **AAS** architecture ([Document 50]) supports **role-based access control (RBAC)** and **attribute-based access control (ABAC)** for granular authorization, where permissions are dynamically assigned based on user roles (e.g., operators, administrators) or operational context (e.g., time-sensitive access). The **Asset Administration Shell Part 1** specification does not explicitly mention **multi-factor authentication (MFA)**, but the **Industrie 4.0 platform** implies integration with **certificate-based authentication** or **token-based systems** (e.g., JWT) for secure API gateways.

    **3. Authentication Systems**:
    The **Functional Mockup Interface (FMI)** ([Document 51]), used for tool-independent exchange of simulation models, suggests reliance on **standardized authentication protocols** (e.g., **X.509 certificates**) for interoperability between simulation tools and digital twins. While not explicitly detailed, the **Thingsboard IoT platform** ([Document 53]) uses **MQTT-SN** (Secure Network) for secure device authentication, implying similar mechanisms in digital twin systems.

    **4. Threat Mitigation Strategies**:
    The **RAMI 4.0** model ([Document 49]) includes a **threat model layer** that addresses cyber threats (e.g., **DDoS attacks, insider threats**) through **hierarchical isolation** (e.g., separating operational and analytical layers). The **Digital Shadows** approach ([Document 42]) for monitoring automation systems suggests **real-time anomaly detection** via **AI-driven monitoring tools** (e.g., **ML-based intrusion detection systems**) to mitigate unauthorized access or malicious actions.

    **5. Fail-Safe Mechanisms**:
    The **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** framework ([Document 43]) for digital twins in manufacturing emphasizes **fail-safe behaviors** through **model transformations** that enforce safety constraints (e.g., **Safety-Critical Systems Engineering (SCSE)** standards). The **MABI robotic arm** ([Document 44]) is cited as a use case where **emergency stop protocols** and **redundant safety mechanisms** (e.g., **limit switches, force sensors**) are critical, implying integration with **digital twin safety monitors** to validate physical constraints in real-time.

    **6. Risk Assessment Approaches**:
    The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** ([Document 50]) incorporates **risk-based classification** of assets (e.g., **criticality levels**) to prioritize security measures. The **Digital Shadows** methodology ([Document 42]) includes **automated risk scoring** for cyber-physical assets, leveraging **graph-based analysis** to identify high-risk connections (e.g., **supply chain dependencies**).

    **7. Safety-Critical System Considerations**:
    The **RAMI 4.0** architecture ([Document 48] and [Document 49]) explicitly defines **safety layers** (e.g., **Layer 0: Physical, Layer 1: Cyber-Physical**) with **hard real-time constraints** (e.g., **latency < 10 ms** for safety-critical signals). The **FMI standard** ([Document 51]) ensures **deterministic execution** of simulation models, critical for **predictive safety validation** in digital twins. The **MABI robotic arm** ([Document 44]) exemplifies **safety-critical applications** where **friction modeling** ([Document 45]) and **dynamic load balancing** are used to prevent mechanical failures.

    **8. Semantic Asset Administration Shell (SAAS)****:
    The **Semantic Asset Administration Shell (SAAS)** ([Document 46] and [Document 47]) introduces **ontology-based security** by defining **semantic roles** (e.g., **owner, operator, auditor**) and **ontology-driven access control**, ensuring that security policies are aligned with **knowledge graphs** for dynamic policy enforcement.
  "
]
